Welcome, welcome everybody. My name is Boss Wauters. I'm the CEO of Chaldini Institute. Great you're all joining here in big numbers and fantastic to see we have an audience here from all over the world. I saw the US, Brazil, Argentina, Croatia, France, Germany, India. Fantastic to see you all joining in today for this exciting program. On behalf of Chaldini Institute, I want to welcome you all to this live webinar and we're going to discuss something very interesting today. The topic of today's webinar is win with small changes that deliver big results. What we call small bigs. What are the smallest things you can do to get big results? Dr. Chaldini will share some very interesting insights today. Not only the intra insights will be very interesting. The great news is you can apply them right away. At the Chaldini Institute, we have three core values. It's science. Everything what we say is based on scientific research. So no guesses or hunches and then application. What we give you, the information we give you, we want it to be applicable for you. And the third is ethics. Just because you can get people to say more yes to you, it doesn't mean it's always the best decision. With an ethical approach, you are able to generate short-term success and long-term success. Fantastic. So that's what we're going to talk about. Before I like to give you some very interesting information and details about the man of the hour, Dr. Chaldini. Perhaps you know this, but Dr. Chaldini is trusted by a lot of organizations all over the world. And that's from non-profit organizations to the Fortune 100 companies. Like for example, the World Economic Forum. Also universities, he's well-known and well-regarded like Harvard, Stanford, Oxford, and of course these huge enterprises like Coca-Cola, KPMG, Google, Microsoft. But what you maybe don't know is that was a very interesting jury, February 2022. And this jury contained of the top entrepreneurs in the world. So some of the juries were Warren Buffett, Mark Hubane, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, and they voted for the best business book of all time. And as you might reckon already, the winner is, for the winner was Dr. Robert Chaldini and his book Influence. So you see how powerful this material is and how many people endorse his life work, which we also have great comments from business leaders. So for example, Richard Branson, Tony Robbins, Daniel Pink. Also on the next slide, we have Guy Kawasaki, Warren Buffett, Tobias Ludke. I love the quote from Tobias Ludke, for example, Influence is one of the two books that made me a billionaire. Imagine how powerful this knowledge, what you're just, what you're going to learn in a moment from the master himself, how powerful and how impactful was for this entrepreneur and how powerful and impactful it could be for you guys as well. And he's not only highly regarded by business leader, also in the scientific community, so on this slide you see two Nobel Prize winners, both Daniel Kahneman as Richard Taylor won the Nobel Prize for Economics. I love the quote, Richard Taylor, if the president had to have one advisor with him at all time, my nomination would be Dr. Robert Chaldini. Wow, what a great comment is that. So fantastic and the man himself is right here. Please give a warm welcome for Dr. Robert Chaldini. Dr. Chaldini, lovely to see you here. Hello, boss. Good to be with you. Fantastic. You're all joining. I see all this applause and hearts in our screen. So I see our audience is also very happy that they see you. Well, Dr. Chaldini, we have created a fantastic program today to share small big, small changes with big outcomes and I want to give the floor to you. Right, and you're exactly right, boss, that we've been focusing on efficiencies within the influence process recently. That is, what are the things that would require the least cost, time, or effort and still produce large, positive effects on your persuasive success. So that's what we'll talk about today and there will be three that we're going to focus on. We originally talked about maybe having four, but when I did a rehearsal of this, it was clear that we could only do three at the speed and at the depth that was appropriate. So here are the three that we will consider. The first is persuasion's most easily correctable mistake. The next is how scarcity, just informing people about a particular form of scarcity, increase sales by 506% and the third will be how to boost conversion with liking. Now, everybody knows that liking works, but you know, a lot of the time we're in, we're online now and how do you get people to like you if they've never met you, never had an experience with you, don't know you. Well, there are some approaches we can take to show how that can be done easily with very little cost. So let's talk about the first of these. The idea that has to do with authority, it's persuasion's most easily correctable mistake has to do with the principle of authority. We all recognize that people prefer to follow the lead of genuine experts, true authorities on the topic. They will, that reduces their uncertainty and they're willing to move forward. So the obvious implication is that, well, before we try to influence anybody, we should honestly inform them of our credentials, our experience, our knowledge on the topic that constitute our authority standing on that topic. And that's true, but it turns out it's not so straightforward. Because in any face-to-face situation, it's a mistake, even if you are that authority, it's a mistake to present your credentials yourself. Because you look like a self-motor as a consequence, a braggart. And research is very clear. If you present that information, that positive information about yourself, you lose credibility in the eyes of the audience. They like you less and they see you as less trustworthy as a consequence. In fact, the research, the newest research shows those people who are highest in competence and expertise take the biggest hit if they introduce themselves with that favorable information. If they're brokers of this positive information about themselves, because they look like self-aggrandizers, well, what can you do if you truly are that expert? Let's say you're in a situation in which you're dealing with a group that is unfamiliar with you. You are an expert on the topic. What do you do to arrange for them to recognize your expertise without having to lose influence points in the form of less likeability and less trustworthiness? The answer is, if that's the case, have somebody else arrange for somebody else to introduce you in that group. It can either be in the best outcome of the leader of that group, or it can be the person who invited you to speak to the group. You should give that person ahead of time, either send it ahead of time or bring a list with you of your strengths, all of the things that are most of interest and feed the goals of the audience that relate to your credentials. Give them that information, let that other person do it. That way the audience is properly informed about your expertise, about your strengths, but you don't have to be the person who provides it and experiences the costs of the interpersonal costs of being a self-promoter in their eyes. Now, if the if the group that you're speaking to is just a single person or two people, that you can handle by sending ahead of your meeting with that person a letter of introduction, in which you say, I'm very much looking forward to meeting with you on Thursday on the topic of X, whatever X is. My background and experience relative to X are as follows, and you can summarize that and then attach either your resume or a link to your, let's say your linked in profile, where they can read more about you before you have that meeting. The key is that with any face-to-face meeting, it's just not acceptable for you to sit down and say, before we begin, let me tell you how great I really am. I mean, it goes against all the rules. People don't like those individuals, even if they're speaking about their genuine credential. So we have to do it differently. We either have somebody else present those strengths or we do it in writing ahead of time, and that solves the problem. Now, what's interesting is that this idea that people should not be in a position of having to present positive information about themselves in a face-to-face situation doesn't just apply to our customers and clients outside of the organization. It also applies inside our organizations with something that is very common in business, and that is the handoff, where we have a customer or client who's come on board, perhaps as a result of contact with a salesperson, and now it's time to transition that customer or client to somebody on our team whose job it is to implement the terms of the contract or to talk about the timing or the delivery or the production of what was promised. We have to have somebody else who takes over for the salesperson. In that situation, before that teammate ever interacts with the client, we need the salesperson or the person who's brought the customer in to credentialize the teammate, to honestly inform that customer or that client of the strengths of the person they are about to interact with because they can't do it themselves. Remember, they will lose influence points in the form of legibility and trust if they have to present that information. If they don't present that information, then the customer or client is ignorant of the true strengths that they have to help them. Let's give an example of how this works then. This was a study that was done in England by our partner Stephen J. Martin who was called in to help a real estate agency in London with a problem they were having. It was that the number of conversions that they got from call ins from people who called was below what they expected. They wanted to turn those into customers rather than just callers. Steve went and walked around and listened to what was going on in the organization. When he got to the receptionist who took the call in, he heard the following. Let's say the customer would call and she would say, are you interested in residential real estate or are you interested in commercial real estate? In what section of London are you most interested? Kensington or Night's Bridge, where are you most interested? She would get that information and then she would say, fine, let me connect you with Jonathan or let me connect you with Janet who was the expert. The problem was she didn't label them. She didn't properly credentialize them as the expert. What Steve asked her to do was to simply say, let me connect you with Jonathan who has the most experience in our entire company with commercial real estate in Night's Bridge or let me connect you with Janet who is our expert at residential real estate in whatever the section of London was. Honestly, provide that information. The consequence of a 15.5% increase in conversions from callers to customers. The thing I love about this is that not only was it a small big, not only was it costless, it required one more breath, a few more words, but those were crucial words that established the authority of the teammate. It was also completely ethical. It was true. It was always true that this receptionist was sending people to the most qualified realtor in the company. She just didn't say so. And the consequence was now they didn't get the kind of conversion that they deserved. Let me suggest how we handle that within our company. Let me read you something from the out of office automatic phone message that our Vice President, Bobbett Gordon uses. And I'll read this verbatim. Thank you for contacting me. This is when she's not in the office. Thank you for contacting me. I'm out of the office today. I'll be back Thursday morning. While I'm gone, for any time sensitive issues, please contact Eileen Cartwright. Eileen is very, and then Eileen's email address. Eileen is very smart, creative, wise, and resourceful. Absolutely true. She knows everything, or she knows where to find it. You couldn't be in better hands. Think of how if you got that message, you would be more likely to go ahead and contact Eileen, who has just been credentialized as something who knows what you want to learn, rather than saying, well, I won't be in the office until Thursday, which might cause a potential customer to say, oh, I need that information. Now, maybe I'll go to a competitor. I'll just call up a competitor who could get me that information right away that I need. You save all of that problem by just honestly providing that information. Okay. So easy to do. We do it all the time, and people love it. They call us and say, well, thank you for that information. That was great, what you said about Eileen. I really wanted to meet her as a consequence. Now, you know, Boss began this segment by introducing me, but Boss is more than just the introducer of me in this segment. He is the author of an award winning book called Online Influence, right? That tells how people can use the persuasion process, become more influential in digitized contexts online. And Boss told me that he has an example of how this kind of thing could be used online. So let me turn it over to Boss to describe what he's learned. Thank you, Bob. And thanks for these kind words of introduction on introducing me. So we walk or talk here, everybody. Yes. So it's interesting. How do you do an introduction in an online environment? Well, after COVID, I think most calendars are packed nowadays with video calls. So what could you do to have an introduction out there just before you meet that person? I always give this advice. Look for a person that would have authority in the eyes of the person you are going to meet. And you could do this in an automated way or do it manually, but let that person draft an introduction message and send that out like five minutes by email to the person you're going to meet, establishing your credibility just before the meeting. We do this and I can promise you this hat. We had great feedback from people that implemented this strategy. But thank you, Bob. All right. So let me go to the next small big. And I'm going to walk us through those slides from the study by Steve. This is the small big it has to do with scarcity and how a particular form of scarcity produced a 509% increase in sales. Now, we all know that scarcity increases the attractiveness of the items that are scarce when people are informed, but something is rare or scarce or dwindling in availability. They wanted more. It was a study done of Australian beef sales that I have some personal knowledge about because it was done by a student of mine. Now, this was not an ordinary student. This was a man who had built a successful business in the Phoenix, Arizona area where I live, importing beef into the United States from Australia and South America and then selling it to large scale supermarket chains. And his business was going so well that he decided that he could take some time off, come back to school and get his PhD in marketing. And I was sitting on his doctoral dissertation committee, his research committee, and he came to my office and he said, you know, I want to do my dissertation research using my own industry, my own salespeople, and my own customers. And we talked about the research that showed, first of all, that people want items, commodities that are scarce, more as a consequence of learning about their scarcity, but also some new research that showed that scarcity doesn't just work for commodities, it also works for information. Information that's not widely available, information that is privileged or exclusive or just brand new and not widely available for people is also more persuasive. He went back to his office armed with this kind of information and set up the following study. He called a meeting of his salespeople who contacted their customers by phone and he said, we have a new allotment of Australian beef that's just come in. I want you to divide your customer lists into thirds at random and call the first third and use the standard appeal that we always use when we have a new allotment of beef to sell, which is we have a new allotment of beef. It is of such and such a quality. We can provide it at such and such a time at such and such a price. How many carloads of beef would you like to purchase? They would sell it in big train carloads. So that was for one third of the group. And what they found was that about they got 10 carloads of beef sold per salesperson using that kind of strategy. There was a second group who learned of the scarcity of that beef. They were told about the allotment and also told that because of certain weather conditions in Australia, there was a likely to be a shortage of Australian beef in the near future. So they added scarcity to the mix. Here's what happened to sales under those circumstances. They jumped to 24 carloads. But we knew that about scarcity. We knew how powerful the principle of scarcity is in spurring people into change. Okay. Well, how about the third group? They were told about the allotment of beef. They were also told about the scarcity of that, of future Australian beef. And then they were told this information about the scarcity of future beef comes to us from our exclusive sources in the Australian National Weather Service. No one else has this information yet. This is the scarcity double whammy, isn't it? Not only is the beef scarce, the information that the beef is scarce is scarce. What happened? 61 carloads of beef were sold under those conditions, under that kind of message. And afterward, this man came to me and said, you know, Dr. Chaldean, all of this was true. There was a pending scarcity of Australian beef. And we got this information from our exclusive contacts in the Australian National Weather Service in Canberra. Contacts that we had cultivated over a long period of time. And he said, those conditions have existed before in the history of my company. I just never knew to use them before because he didn't know what the research said. Well, now you know what the research says about scarcity and about exclusivity of information. So what's the implication for you? When, because you may have certain kinds of contacts that can give you information, maybe send you a white paper or an article before it's even published, or you go to a conference and hear a talk from somebody before that information is widely available beyond the people who happen to be in that room at the conference. And you have somebody who you would like to persuade in your direction, and that this new information that you have is directly relevant to making your case. Two things. First, move immediately with that new information. Information is not like wine. It doesn't get better with age. It's like bread. We want it when it's fresher, when it's warmer. We want to take it in under those circumstances. So the first thing you do is move, get on the computer, get on the phone, get in people's offices immediately with that new information. And secondly, tell them that it's new. Tell them that this is not widely available, honestly, because it's not even published yet, or you just got back from a conference in Chicago where you heard this at a national meeting and only the people in this room heard about this. Tell them that this is exclusive information. Watch what happens when you tell them, when you say, what I'm about to tell you is brand new information. You're the first person I'm telling about this, or you're one of the first here's what happens. They lean in to the next thing you say. And then you provide that favorable information about your position. And as we've seen in that Australian beef study, you significantly increase the power of that message that you are sending. Honestly, because you truly do have information that's limited in its availability. I know that something like this happened to me back a while ago when my financial advisor, I named a great guy named Larry Erickson, he would call me after he had just been in a meeting with the expert in his brokerage on a particular topic that I had an interest in, maybe because it was part of my portfolio. He would call me on the phone, he would say, Bob, I just got out of a meeting with our expert who's given us the latest news and latest advice for how we should proceed on this particular topic. All right. Would you like to schedule a meeting with me to hear about this? Or would you like to tell me now? And here's what I would say. Now, Larry, now, now, I want this information when it's new, when it's not widely available. And then when he would give me the information, at the end, and I put down the phone, I would see there was a phone-sized impression in the side of my face because I was pressing that phone so hard into my face and my ear, I wanted that information more when it's new. We can do this. First of all, we should cultivate sources like as happened in that Australian beef study. Remember, the business owner had cultivated people in the relationships with people in the Australian National Weather Service for a long series of times. So he had those connections. He could give them a call and say, well, what's new? Right? Even before it was widely publicized. So go to meetings where you can get new information. If you get articles or white papers across your desk that are just published or haven't even been published yet because you've gotten a preprint of it, get that kind of information and then use that exactly that information that this is new or not yet published yet. People will be eager to respond to it. Okay. Let's now talk about the third of the principles. And this one has to do with boosting conversions through the liking principle. You know, we've all learned that liking leads to yes. There's just very little doubt that that's the case. When people are in a relationship where they like one another, they want to work together. And I can't think of a single influence program or many sales programs that I've either observed or been participating in in that fail to make this point. In fact, in many of them, they say that the number one rule of sales is get your customer to like you. If they like you, they'll want to do business with you and you will get more success. And I don't doubt that at all. But I do doubt its status as the number one rule of sales. I think that one is the number two rule. Here's the number one rule of sales. Come to like your customer and show them that you like them. That's when people will move in your direction. Why? Because they're safe there. They're safe in the hands of an expert. I hope you by this time established your authority and expertise. Who likes them? Their interests are served by people who like them more than just by people that they like. If I'm going to do business and I'm going to buy a big item, I want somebody who likes me, who give me counsel, who give me direction in the best way for me to optimize my choices because they like me. They'll be willing to do that. So what I insist on is finding ways for us before we begin the influence process to show people that we like them. And here is the easiest way, the smallest way to do so. It's a smile. The research is very clear on this. People feel closer to us. They feel a connection with us. If they receive a smile first, I heard a researcher describing a survey that he had done. I heard him, it was on a radio interview. And he said, you know these kinds of offices where you go in like professional offices, doctors, debtists, chiropractors, financial investment firms, where you go into the office and there's a desk. And you meet somebody at the desk and then he or she sends you to the person that you're interested in. This researcher asks, what is the thing you most want from that person at the desk? It wasn't evidence of competence or experience or intelligence or attention. It was a smile. People wanted a smile first so that they got a feeling of a positive human connection, of liking for them. So one thing we should always do, and the research supports this, is be sure that we find a reason to smile honestly at the people that we are interacting with. And the research is even more surprising as to the power of that smile, in that it shows that sometimes you don't need a physical smile to produce this effect. All you need is the shape of a smile. A line with upturned edges. Let me give you some evidence that was done in a study of, it was a consumer research study in which people were asked to rate the likelihood that they would choose a particular shampoo when it was presented in one of four different bottles. If you look at these bottles, you can see that they are alike. They are identical except for the line at the bottom. The one on the left has an upturned smile kind of shaped line. Then there's a neutral line, then there's no line, and finally there's one that looks downturned like a frown. And then the researchers asked these individuals, which of these bottles would you be most likely to purchase in the store? Let me show you the results. No question at all. The one that had the upturned curved line was significantly more likely to be selected than any of the others. And notice that the one that was least likely was the one that imitated a frown. People are sensitive to even the shapes of the lines in this instance. And they didn't even realize why they were making the choices when researchers asked them, why did they choose this? They said, I don't know. It was getting in kind of unconsciously, non-consciously. So what's the implication? Well, one is if you look at the logos of many powerful, successful companies, take a look at these. Amazon, Dan and Yoplay, Conagra, all of these big companies and even some smaller ones, Kraft Foods and so on, there's another big one. They have all by virtue of surveys they've done, interviews they've done, focus groups they've done for months, at thousands of dollars, they've all hit upon this upturned line inclusion in their logos. They spent thousands of dollars to come upon the right logo. We do the same thing. We in the Chaldeany Institute have a smile next to our logo. Gets you their smile and it didn't cost us a cent to come to that recognition, to that realization of what to put there. Because we know the research, we know the behavioral science research, that's all you need. So you can do the same thing and I think that's the case for not just your logo, but your packaging, your business cards, anything that you present to the public, why not put a little smile, curved line there because you do want to show people that you like them. You want people who come to your site, for example, to show them that you are positively inclined toward them. Okay, now besides that, there's one other small big I'd like to tell you about that increases the liking process and increases the likelihood that people will want to do business with you because you've shown them that you like them. What else besides a smile could you provide? And the answer comes from a study of 6,700 online commercial sites where they did A, B tests of one sort or another and included one feature or removed that feature from the site to see if it increased conversions, things like free shipping, a financial reason, things like a search function, a technological reason, even a sales reason like including a call to action line. They tested the presence or the absence of each of those things and looked to see whether they made a difference. I'm happy to tell you that the ones at the top were the principles of influence that we talk about in the Chaldey Institute, but one of them was the liking principle. Well, how do you increase liking online? It was simplicity itself. It was to include a welcoming message on the landing page of the site. If there was a message saying, we're so glad you're here, welcome to our site. We're so glad to be able to help you. Let me read what ours looks like. You can see it there. Welcome to the Chaldey Institute. I'm very pleased you're considering one of our programs. My team and I will be happy to provide you everything you need to succeed. Imagine how easy it would be to simply include a welcoming message onto your site because what that study showed is that if there was a welcoming message, they significantly increased the percentage of people who visited who would become customers. It's the liking principle. I'm going to predict that small things like a smile and a welcome are going to become even more impactful in the future because technology is separating us. It's separating us from one another. We work separately. We shop separately. We entertain ourselves separately from one another and people are going to want those connections. They're going to yearn for them to a greater extent because we're being walled off from one another by technology. The more we can include them in our messaging, the better our results will be. That's the set of studies I wanted to talk about with small bigs that led to the kind of success we were hoping for. I'm going to turn things back over to Bas who will give you some more information about the Chaldey Institute if you wanted to learn more at greater depth. Thank you. Thanks Dr. Chaldey for this great explanation and your amazing way of presenting this information. I saw all these fantastic comments in the chat and a lot of applause now. Thank you all for joining us here. Fantastic. We love that everybody came in such big numbers from all over the world. Dr. Chaldey, thanks for sharing all your wisdom and practical applications with the audience. Well thank you Bas. I enjoyed it a lot and I hope other people had been able to benefit and increase their persuasive success as a result. Fantastic. Thank you. Thanks everybody for joining. Remember Chaldey Institute gets you there. See you soon. Bye bye.
